creative_brief: Hero image: close-up of hands holding a crate of mixed produce with visible farm labels — earthy, warm light, slightly imperfect vegetables. Below the fold, candid photography of real partner farms (Kempsey landscape, Bundarra pigs, fishing boats on the South Coast). Muted greens and warm neutrals. No stock photography. Typography in sentence case, generous whitespace. The feeling should be: honest, grounded, purposeful — not polished retail.
headline: What if the grocery store you wanted actually needed you to exist?
subhead: A farm-direct grocery store is trying to cut out the middlemen, pay farmers fairly, and feed your family food you can trace to the paddock. It won't happen without people like you behind it.
hero_copy:
You've thought about it. Standing in the aisle, reading labels that tell you nothing. 'Product of Australia' — from where? Grown how? By whom?

You're not apathetic. You just don't have a real option.

FarmThru is building one — a grocery store with no warehouses, no wholesalers, no middlemen. Food goes from farms like Rachel Ward's regenerative property in Kempsey, NSW, straight to a hub you collect from.

But a store like this doesn't get built by venture capital chasing margins. It gets built by the people who'll actually shop there.

sections.heading: The system wasn't built for you to ask questions
sections.body:
Supermarkets move food an average of 2,400km before it reaches the shelf. Along the way, it passes through warehouses, wholesalers, and distributors — each one adding cost and removing information.

By the time you pick it up, the farmer's name is gone. The farming method is gone. The story of the land is gone. All that's left is a barcode and a price.

FarmThru's supply chain works differently. Farm to hub to you. Around 15–20 partner farms and suppliers across NSW — like Bundarra Farm for pork, Collins Wild Caught for South Coast seafood, Paris Creek Farms for carbon-neutral biodynamic dairy, and Farmer Brown for eggs.

Every product traces back to a real place and a real person. Because you never stopped wanting to know. You just stopped being able to find out.

sections.heading: This isn't a subscription box — it's a grocery store with a conscience
sections.body:
No membership fees. No lock-in contracts. You order online when you need groceries, and collect from the Brookvale hub Monday to Friday. Bulk buying keeps prices fair — for you and the farmer.

FarmThru exists because the people who grow regenerative food deserve a direct line to the people who want to eat it. No unnecessary middlemen skimming margin. No pressure to shrink-wrap the origin story out of your dinner.

This is food you can stand behind. As their tagline puts it: "We'll show you where your food comes from."

sections.heading: Own a piece of the store you wish existed
sections.body:
FarmThru is opening an equity crowdfunding campaign — which means everyday people can become part-owners of a food system that actually works.

This isn't charity. It's a chance to vote for change with your wallet and be a partner in something you believe should exist. If you've ever said "someone should build a better grocery store" — this is your moment to be part of it.

Join the waitlist now with a fully refundable $5 deposit. No commitment beyond that. You'll be first to know when the campaign goes live, and first to decide if it's right for you.

Investing in early-stage businesses carries risk, including the possibility of losing your entire investment. This is not financial advice. Read the offer document carefully before deciding.

sections.heading: Compliance
sections.body: FarmThru's equity crowdfunding campaign, when live, will be conducted in accordance with Australian securities law. Any investment carries risk, including the risk of losing your entire investment. Past performance is not a reliable indicator of future performance. This page is not a product disclosure statement or financial advice. You should consider your own financial situation and read the offer document in full before making any investment decision.
